message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 31
Recent Green phase: No implementation change — three characterization tests passed (cap 2000 single claim →1400/600, cap exhaustion across two claims →600/0, high-ench payout 350.5 rounded down →350). The claim cap/rounding behavior is now well covered.

Refactor while keeping all tests green (`pnpm test`). Evaluate naming, Four Rules, APP mass across the whole file. Apply an improvement if warranted; otherwise document why none. Remaining upcoming tests: two-swords multi-entry deductible, "more damage entries than covered → reject", and three error cases (unknown quote item type, claim item not in policy, negative damage amount). Do NOT implement these yet, but you may structure to ease them (e.g. itemFor's `!` will become a real membership check). Keep all current behavior identical.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 31 tests pass. Now let me analyze the code systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

This file is already well-factored from prior refactor cycles. Names are strong: `sumBy`, `percentOf`, `conditionalPercent`, `basePremiumForType`, `enchantmentLevel`, `itemSurcharge`, `policyAdjustments`, `reimbursement`, `damagePayout`, `itemFor`. Each clearly reveals intent.

One naming concern flagged in the prompt: `itemFor` uses a non-null assertion `!`. The comment already documents this as intentional. The name `itemFor` reads well ("the policy item for this damage").

## Step 2: APP Mass — area of interest

The prompt highlights `itemFor`'s `!` becoming a real membership check, and the upcoming "claim item not in policy → error" test. The current `itemFor` does a `.find(...)!`. Let me look at whether there's an improvement that eases the upcoming work without changing behavior.

Looking at the structure, the code is genuinely clean. Let me focus on a concrete, behavior-preserving improvement. Examining `quote`:

The `quote` function inlines `policyBase` and passes it to multiple helpers (`sumItemSurcharges`, `policyAdjustments`). That's fine. Let me look at `policyAdjustments` — it takes `customer`, `quoteIndex`, `policyBase`. This is a reasonable grouping.

Let me consider the one spot that's slightly off: **Rule 3 (duplication)** between `itemBasePremium` and the `count * basePremiumForType(type)` in `typeGroupPremium`. Both express "base price of a type", and both already route through `basePremiumForType`. No duplication there.

Let me reconsider `itemFor`. The find predicate `(item) => item.type === damage.itemType` couples to `damage.itemType`. Consider whether extracting an `insures` predicate clarifies intent and eases the upcoming membership-check test.

Let me measure the current `itemFor` and `claim` region precisely.

**Current `itemFor` (lines 207-208):**
```
const itemFor = (policyItems, damage) =>
  policyItems.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType)!;
```
- Bindings: `policyItems`, `damage`, `item` = 3
- Invocations: `.find` = 2
- The arrow predicate body: comparison `item.type === damage.itemType` — property accesses + `===`. In APP, member access/comparison count as invocations conceptually; conservatively count `===` as 1 invocation = 2.
- Total ≈ 3 + 2 + 2 = 7

This is already minimal. Extracting an `insures(item, damage)` predicate would *add* an invocation (mass up) for marginal clarity. Given Rule 2 trumps APP, is the clarity gain real? The predicate is short and readable inline. Extraction would help the upcoming reject-test (a single membership concept), but the prompt says **do not implement those yet**. Adding the abstraction now with no consumer would violate Rule 4 (fewest elements) — a speculative abstraction.

## Step 3: Apply Rules — looking for a genuine, non-speculative improvement

Let me re-examine `damagePayout` and `reimbursement` naming against the full set of claim tests. The high-enchantment claim clause is 50% reimbursement. `reimbursement` returns the pre-deductible amount; `damagePayout` applies the deductible and floors at 0. These read correctly.

Let me check `claim`: it computes `requested` via `sumBy`, then `Math.floor(Math.min(requested, remainingCap))`. The rounding test (350.5 → 350) confirms floor-after-cap. This is correct and minimal.

Let me look closely at whether `Math.floor` should be inside the per-damage sum or outside. The rounding test uses a single damage of 350.5, capped at 1600, floor → 350. Both placements give the same result here, but they could differ for multi-damage fractional cases. Current placement (floor the total) is a single, clear rounding point. No change warranted without a test distinguishing them.

The strongest candidate improvement: **`conditionalPercent`'s parameter order**. It's `(applies, amount, percent)`. Reads as "conditional percent: applies? amount, percent". Callers read naturally. Fine.

Let me reconsider `policyAdjustments`. It computes `firstInsuranceSurcharge(policyBase) - loyaltyDiscount(...) - followUpDiscount(...)`. The naming "adjustments" with a signed-net comment is good.

## Decision

Let me look for one concrete win: the `quote` premium expression recomputes nothing redundantly, but `sumBasePremiums` is called once for `policyBase` — good. 

Let me examine `enchantmentLevel` usage vs a subtle duplication: the two enchantment thresholds (`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MIN_LEVEL = 5` for quote, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_MIN_LEVEL = 8` for claim) are distinct constants — correct, not duplication.

I'll apply one improvement that is real and non-speculative: the predicate inside `itemFor` compares `item.type === damage.itemType`. There's a parallel comparison concept already present — `countByType` and `find` both reason about item type. But the cleanest genuine improvement is naming the **damage→type relationship** without adding a speculative membership API.

Let me verify by checking: is there any actual duplication of the `.type === ...itemType` comparison anywhere else?

The comparison appears once. No duplication.

Given how thoroughly factored this file already is, I'll make one targeted, behavior-preserving improvement that improves intent-revelation (Rule 2) **and** eases the upcoming "claim item not in policy → error" test exactly as the prompt invited ("itemFor's `!` will become a real membership check"): extract the damage→policy-item matching predicate into a named `insures` helper. This is not purely speculative — the predicate is currently an anonymous lambda mixing two concepts (iteration via `find` and the matching rule), and naming the matching rule makes `itemFor` read as "find the policy item that insures this damage."

Let me measure before/after.

**Before — `itemFor` (mass):**
```
const itemFor = (policyItems, damage) =>
  policyItems.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType)!;
```
- Bindings: `policyItems`, `damage`, `item` = 3
- Invocations: `.find` (2) + `===` comparison (2) = 4
- Loop: `.find` iteration — already counted the call; treat the predicate as a binding-level comparison.
- Total ≈ 7

**After:**
```
const insures = (item, damage) => item.type === damage.itemType;
const itemFor = (policyItems, damage) =>
  policyItems.find((item) => insures(item, damage))!;
```
- `insures`: bindings `item`, `damage` (2) + `===` invocation (2) = 4
- `itemFor`: bindings `policyItems`, `damage`, `item` (3) + `.find` (2) + `insures(...)` call (2) = 7
- Total ≈ 11

This *raises* APP mass (7 → 11) by adding an invocation. Per the priority rules, **Rule 2 (clarity) trumps APP** — but only when the clarity gain is real and the abstraction earns its place (Rule 4). Here the inline predicate `item.type === damage.itemType` is already perfectly clear, and `insures` has only one caller. Adding it now is a **speculative abstraction with no current consumer** that increases element count and mass for no behavioral or readability gain that the inline form doesn't already provide.

Reverting that idea. Let me reconsider whether *any* change improves the code on its own merits today.

Looking once more, every helper has a clear single responsibility, names reveal intent, there's no duplication, constants are well-named with explanatory comments, and APP mass is already minimal throughout. The two `it.todo` claim tests and three error tests are upcoming — but structuring for them now (membership checks, validation guards) would add unused branches/abstractions, violating Rule 4 and the prompt's "keep all current behavior identical."

Let me do a final verification that the McCabe complexity per function is already minimal (no function has nested conditionals or loops that could be flattened) by re-scanning the conditionals.

Every conditional is a single ternary (or `??` default) at the leaf of a one-line helper — McCabe complexity of 2 per function at most, no nesting. There is nothing to flatten.

I've completed a thorough evaluation across all Four Rules and APP. Let me confirm tests are still green (no changes made, but verifying the baseline holds).

content.text:
Refactoring evaluation complete. All 31 tests remain green.

## Refactoring Evaluation — `src/claim-office.ts`

I evaluated the whole file against the Four Rules (priority order) and measured APP mass on candidate changes. **No refactoring was applied** because every change I considered would have made the code worse on its own merits today. Detailed reasoning below.

### Step 1 — Naming (first priority)
All helpers reveal intent clearly: `sumBy`, `percentOf`, `conditionalPercent`, `basePremiumForType`, `enchantmentLevel`, `itemSurcharge`, `policyAdjustments`, `reimbursement`, `damagePayout`, `itemFor`. Each name matches what it does across the full current test set. No rename warranted.

### Step 2/3 — Candidate I measured and rejected: extract an `insures` predicate from `itemFor`
The prompt invited structuring so `itemFor`'s `!` becomes a real membership check. I measured it:
- Before — `itemFor` with inline predicate `item.type === damage.itemType`: APP mass ≈ 7
- After — adding `const insures = (item, damage) => item.type === damage.itemType` and calling it: APP mass ≈ 11 (an added invocation)

This **raises** mass, and the clarity gain is nil — the inline comparison is already perfectly readable. With only one caller, `insures` is a speculative abstraction (violates Rule 4, fewest elements) with no current consumer. Rule 2 would override APP only if clarity actually improved; it doesn't. Rejected. When the "claim item not in policy → error" test lands, that test will justify turning the `.find(...)!` into a guarded lookup — but adding the seam now, with no behavior to drive it, would be implementing ahead.

### Rule 3 — Duplication
The `.type === ...itemType` comparison appears exactly once (verified). The two enchantment thresholds (`HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MIN_LEVEL = 5` for quotes, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_MIN_LEVEL = 8` for claims) are genuinely distinct values, not duplication. Both per-item and per-group pricing already route through the single `basePremiumForType`. No duplication to remove.

### Rule 4 / McCabe
Every conditional is a single leaf-level ternary or `??` default inside a one-line helper (max McCabe ≈ 2, zero nesting). `countByType` holds the only loop, and it is already minimal. Nothing to flatten or inline.

### Why none was applied
This file has been incrementally refactored across prior cycles and is already at a local optimum: clear names, no duplication, minimal APP mass per helper, flat complexity, well-documented constants. The only structural changes available (membership seam, validation guards) are driven by the upcoming `it.todo` tests, and adding them now would introduce unused branches/abstractions — violating Rule 4 and the instruction to keep current behavior identical.

**Tests:** 31 passed, 5 todo — all green, no file changes.

Relevant file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_07-28-07_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
